text.heading: Fed Govt to stop funding rural electrification
text.p: From John Ofikhenua, Umuahia
THE Federal Government is to stop funding rural electrification projects.
Under the new policy, it will pass the responsibility to state and local governments.

Managing Director Rural Electrification Agency (REA) Mr. Sam Gekpe said yesterday in Umuahia that President Umar Musa Yar'Adua threatened to stop funding the projects because investment in the sector did not match the returns.
Gekpe, during a courtesy visit by REA management to Abia State Governor, Chief Theodore Orji, said the president had noted that the projects were inconsistent and poorly managed.
He quoted the President as saying that he would be relieved to let the state and local governments take over the funding of the projects.

He said: "In recent times, there has been a controversy , the President is of the opinion that rural electrification should cease to be the concern of the Federal Government and that it should actually concern the state and local governments.

"He is actually threatening to stop funding these projects. One, because of the performance they talked about in the previous years and how the projects had been managed; and the second aspect is the inconsistency in the method of funding this project."
Gekpe, however, added that the position of the National Assembly differed from Yar'Adua’s.
The National Assembly, he said, believes that the three tiers of government should collectively fund the projects.
According to Gekpe, the alternative way to fund the projects is by pooling resources under the rural electrification fund arrangement.
He said REA had proposed to raise funds locally for specific projects, but stakeholders are scared due to the history of management of funds in the industry.

Under the proposed arrangement, a promoter will execute a project, with an assurance that he will recoup his investment, while the community where the project is located will provide the land and security for the installation.
Alternatively, a private investor could also fund 60 percent of the project while state and local council contribute 20 percent each of the cost.

Gekpe said: "The land can be evaluated and converted as equity and that makes the communities part-owners of the scheme and when that happens, and for non-viable projects, we feel well that where the private partner is not interested, the burden will be borne by the state and the agency. Here we will be proposing a partnership of 70 percent contribution from both the state and local governments and community while REA provides 30 percent."

While seeking the governor's support for the collaborative funding of rural electrification projects in Abia State, Gekpe said there were 46 REA projects in Abia, adding that 17 of the projects had been completed.
He advised the state government to invest in solar energy for the electrification of its rural communities.
Orji thanked REA for the 17 projects it had executed, adding that since power projects are capital intensive, the cost should not be borne alone by the Federal Government.
The state, according to Orji, has engaged the service of Geometric Company to provide electricity in rural and urban areas.

"The company is asking the Federal Government to grant it tax waiver for equipment ordered for building the turbine for the project and if we are assured that Shell will keep supplying gas, it will be nice since it will lessen the burden on the Federal Government.
"We are doing our best to reactivate all the rural projects in Abia and we have already released 150 transformers to our rural dwellers," he added.
